title: It Gets Better

chord_progression.theory_notes: Bb natural minor: Bb C Db Eb F Gb Ab. The VI (Gbmaj7) is the soulful Kettama signature — a major 7th chord on the 6th degree gives warmth and lift. III (Dbmaj) is the stable middle ground. i (Bbm) is the root resolution. The loop VI→III→i never lands on i as a 'final' resolution — it keeps cycling, giving the track a floating, perpetual quality. This is a core Kettama technique: always implying resolution without fully arriving.

structure.description: Bass + guitar bass + rave lead, no kick yet
Kick enters (4-on-floor), sub + rave lead 2 at bar 25
Top loop + clap + pad enter at 37, pluck at 41, white noise at 45
Everything strips back — piano only + sub drop audio. Dramatic negative space.
Full chord stack enters: chord + main chord + main chord 2 + lead + lead 2 + piano + main pad + mid bass
Kick + top loop return. Drop pad adds energy. Sub variation at bar 89.
Kick drops, one-bar stabs (bars 121–134), snare 909 from bar 129, drop pad from bar 125
Full arrangement returns for final run. Piano re-enters at bars 135, 145, 149, 153, 157.

tracks.name: bass
tracks.role: Main bass — walks Bb minor scale (F#, C#, D#, A#, F, G# across sections)

tracks.name: mid bass
tracks.role: Mid-range bass layer in main sections

tracks.name: sub
tracks.role: Sub bass — enters at bar 25, drops in piano section, returns at drop

tracks.name: piano
tracks.role: Piano — centrepiece of the track, appears in drops and breakdowns

tracks.name: drop pad
tracks.role: Drop-specific pad, adds fullness to drops

tracks.name: main chord
tracks.role: Main chord layer (same progression, fuller voicing)

tracks.name: clap 2
tracks.role: Audio clap hit at section transitions

tracks.name: Crash
tracks.role: Crash cymbal at major section transitions

tracks.name: sub drop
tracks.role: Sub bass audio sample in piano section (49–53)

bass_pattern.key_notes: All in Bb natural minor scale (Bb=A#, Db=C#, Eb=D#, F, Gb=F#, Ab=G#)
bass_pattern.description: Bass walks through the Bb minor scale across a 2-bar phrase. F#(Gb) is the bass root for the Gbmaj7 chord section, C#(Db) for the Dbmaj section, A#(Bb) for the Bbm section. Each note is subdivided with quick 16th-note articulations (0.25, 0.5 beat durations) for a bouncy, driving feel.
bass_pattern.technique: Each bass note uses a quick-attack articulation: 0.25 beat pickup → 0.5 beat held → repeat. Creates rhythmic momentum rather than held notes.

key_techniques: VI→III→i chord loop — always cycling, never fully resolving — Kettama's floating minor feel
Triple chord layering: chord + main chord + main chord 2 (three instances of same progression for density)
Piano as structural pivot — appears alone at bar 49 to strip the energy, then returns in drops
4-on-the-floor kick with clap on 2 and 4 — more dance/house feel vs the open-hat UK garage approach
Rave lead enters before kick (bar 17) — bass energy before the beat creates anticipation
One-bar stab technique in breakdown (bars 121–134) — track stutters to nothing bar by bar
Sub drops at the start of drops for physical impact
theory_notes: It Gets Better is built on Bb natural minor at 140 BPM. The chord progression Gbmaj7→Dbmaj→Bbm is the VI→III→i in Bb minor — a soulful, perpetually-unresolved loop that defines Kettama's melodic style. Analytically: natural minor gives you 7 chords (i, iidim, III, iv, v, VI, VII). Kettama uses the three 'warmest' ones. The major 7th on the VI chord (Gbmaj7) is the signature sound — it adds a dreamy, jazz-inflected quality that lifts the track above straight minor-key darkness. Compare to MPH Raw which uses no chords at all — this track shows the opposite approach: dense harmonic layering over a driving beat.
vs_mph_raw: MPH Raw: no chords, phrygian tension, E minor pedal note, aggression through rhythm. Kettama It Gets Better: rich chord progression, Bb minor, VI→III→i loop, emotional complexity through harmony. Two completely different approaches to UK garage at similar BPMs (132 vs 140).

elements_to_transplant: VI→III→i chord loop (transpose to any minor key)
Triple chord layer technique (same chords on 3 separate MIDI tracks for thickness)
Piano-only breakdown for maximum tension release
